content.text: Inner-Source Contribution Model for Claude Tools
Platform teams that own shared Claude libraries — the internal SDK wrappers, auth middleware, and prompt registry — can't realistically keep pace with every feature request from 30+ product squads.
An inner-source model solves this by treating those libraries like open-source projects internally: any engineer can file an RFC, fork the repo, submit a pull request, and have it reviewed by

who own the quality bar. The result is faster delivery of platform features, shared ownership of production reliability, and explicit career recognition for engineers who invest in shared infrastructure.

attrs.alt: Inner-Source Contribution Lifecycle with roles and CI gates

content.text: Every inner-source contribution to a Claude platform library passes through five phases. Understanding each phase — and the artifacts it produces — prevents wasted effort and late-stage rejections.
Contributors find contribution opportunities in two places: the
(a "Help Wanted" tag on library issues) and the
file, which identifies who to contact before starting work. Skipping discovery leads to duplicate PRs and misaligned designs.

# Find all Claude-platform repos with Help Wanted issues
gh issue list --label "help-wanted" --repo acme/acme-claude-sdk

# Identify codeowners for a module
cat acme-claude-sdk/CODEOWNERS
# Example output:
# /src/auth/       @platform/core-team @alice
# /src/retry/      @platform/core-team
# /src/prompts/    @platform/core-team @bob @carol

For any change larger than a 20-line bug fix, open an RFC (Request for Comments) issue using the library's issue template. The RFC must state the problem, proposed solution, API surface change, and evaluation criteria. Product Owners have 5 business days to approve, reject, or request design changes before any code is written.

<!-- .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/rfc.md -->
## Problem
<!-- What breaks or is missing without this change? -->

## Proposed Solution
<!-- API sketch or algorithm description -->

## Public API Changes
<!-- New functions/classes, changed signatures, removals -->
# Example:
# - ADD: `AcludeClaude.with_fallback(model: str, max_retries: int) -> AcludeClaude`
# - CHANGE: `AcludeClaude.complete()` now raises `QuotaExceededError` instead of returning None

## Evaluation Criteria
<!-- How will we know this works correctly? Evals, benchmarks, or test cases -->

## Backwards Compatibility
<!-- None / Additive / Breaking (requires major version bump) -->

Once the RFC is approved, contributors fork the repo (or create a branch per the team's preference), implement the feature, and open a pull request. The PR must include unit tests, an integration test covering the happy path, and — for any change that affects model output — an eval diff showing before/after latency and quality scores.
All Claude platform libraries require at least one

approval before merge. Trusted committers are engineers designated by the Product Owner — typically two to four per library — who have demonstrated deep familiarity with the module's invariants. They review for API ergonomics, backward compatibility, test coverage, and eval regression. Guest contributors may not self-approve their own PRs.

Trusted committers are responsible for the long-term maintainability of what they approve. If a contribution introduces a breaking change or performance regression in production, the trusted committer who approved it is on-call alongside the contributor.
Reviewer checks: does the code match the approved RFC? If not, the RFC must be updated first.
API surface: new public symbols must have a docstring and appear in CHANGELOG.md.
Test coverage: branch coverage ≥ 85% on new code paths (enforced by CI).
Eval regression: no metric may degrade more than 2% from the main branch baseline.
Security scan: Semgrep and Bandit must both exit 0.
When all gates pass and a trusted committer approves, the PR is merged via

to preserve authorship in the commit graph. The CHANGELOG entry must credit the contributor's team. Within 30 days, the platform team ships a minor release that includes the change, and the contributor's manager receives an automated Slack notification linking the shipped feature to the engineer's contribution.
Inner-source repositories follow a layout that makes contribution expectations explicit and discoverable. The most important files are:

acme-claude-sdk/
├── CONTRIBUTING.md          # contribution process, RFC link, contact list
├── CODEOWNERS               # per-module trusted committer assignments
├── CHANGELOG.md             # versioned entry for every merged change
├── .github/
│   ├── ISSUE_TEMPLATE/
│   │   ├── bug_report.md
│   │   └── rfc.md           # RFC template (required for non-trivial changes)
│   └── pull_request_template.md
├── src/
│   └── acme_claude/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── client.py        # AcludeClaude wrapper
│       ├── auth.py          # acme-auth integration
│       ├── retry.py         # contributed by guest: @dan, team-search
│       └── prompts.py       # prompt registry client
├── tests/
│   ├── unit/
│   └── integration/
└── evals/
    └── regression/          # eval suite run by CI on every PR
The CI pipeline enforces every quality gate automatically. Contributors cannot bypass any step — the merge button is disabled until all checks are green and a trusted committer has approved.

# .github/workflows/pr-checks.yml
name: PR Checks
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install ruff mypy && ruff check . && mypy src/

  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]" && pytest --cov=acme_claude --cov-fail-under=85

  security:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install semgrep bandit
      - run: semgrep --config=auto src/
      - run: bandit -r src/ -ll

  eval-regression:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run eval suite
        run: |
          pip install -e ".[dev]"
          python evals/regression/run_evals.py --baseline main --threshold 0.02
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.EVAL_API_KEY }}
Not all contributions require the same level of scrutiny. The tier system scales the review effort to the risk level of the change.
Every inner-source library must ship a
that makes the contribution process self-evident. Below is the canonical template used by all Claude platform libraries.

# Contributing to acme-claude-sdk

Thank you for helping improve the shared Claude infrastructure.

## Before You Start

1. Check [open issues](../../issues) for existing work on your idea.
2. For changes beyond bug fixes, open an RFC issue first using the
   [RFC template](.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/rfc.md). Wait for approval
   before writing code.
3. Contact the relevant CODEOWNER (see CODEOWNERS) to discuss scope.

## Setting Up

```bash
git clone git@github.com:acme/acme-claude-sdk.git
cd acme-claude-sdk
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -e ".[dev]"
```

## Making Changes

- One logical change per PR. Combine unrelated fixes into separate PRs.
- Tests are required. PRs without tests will be returned for revision.
- Update CHANGELOG.md under the `[Unreleased]` section.
- Run `ruff check . && mypy src/ && pytest` locally before pushing.

## PR Checklist

- [ ] RFC approved (for non-trivial changes)
- [ ] Unit tests added / updated
- [ ] Integration test covers the happy path
- [ ] CHANGELOG.md updated
- [ ] `ruff`, `mypy`, `bandit` all pass locally

## Trusted Committers

| Module       | Trusted Committers               |
|--------------|----------------------------------|
| auth/        | @alice, @platform/core-team      |
| retry/       | @bob, @platform/core-team        |
| prompts/     | @carol, @platform/core-team      |

Trusted committers review within 3 business days.
Inner-source contributions are tracked in the platform engineering
, which is visible to engineering managers during performance reviews. Repeated high-quality contributions lead to nomination as a

— a designation that carries formal authority over a module and is recognized in the engineering leveling rubric. The nomination process requires sponsorship from the existing trusted committer cohort and approval from the platform Product Owner.
→ any engineer who submits a merged PR to a platform library
→ 3+ merged PRs with positive review feedback
→ nominated, maintains merge authority for a module
→ full-time owners of the library, run the release train

The ledger is read by the performance calibration tool during semi-annual reviews. A Tier 2 or Tier 3 contribution to a platform library consumed by 10+ teams is treated as equivalent impact to shipping a feature in a product used by the same audience.
PR rejected after weeks of work
No RFC opened; design mismatch discovered at review
Always open RFC and get approval before coding
New retry logic changed latency distribution
Contributor pushed to main branch directly
Use fork-and-PR workflow; CI enforces CODEOWNERS review
Check CHANGELOG tier rules; coordinate with core team for T3
CHANGELOG not updated; commit squashed without author
Update CHANGELOG; use --no-ff merge to preserve authorship
The inner-source contribution model enables any engineer to improve Claude platform libraries without waiting for the platform team's sprint cycle. The
— discover, propose, develop, review, merge — each have clear artifacts and owners. The
scales review overhead to change risk.
are the quality gatekeepers, and the
ensures that investment in shared infrastructure is visible and rewarded. CI gates enforce correctness automatically, making the human review faster and more focused on design and maintainability.
Open an RFC before writing code for any change beyond a bug fix.
All PRs must include tests and pass the full CI gate suite.
Trusted committers review and merge; guest contributors cannot self-approve.
Contributions are credited in CHANGELOG.md and the platform ledger.
Repeated high-quality contributions lead to trusted committer nomination.
